text: {"matches":[{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":5,"rationale":"Claim 0 describes egg‑white composition while claim 5 gives whole‑egg calories and protein, different components and metrics."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":0,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"Claim 0 focuses on egg‑white nutrients; claim 2 reports whole‑egg fat and cholesterol percentages."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":5,"toIndex":8,"rationale":"Claim 5 provides total calories and protein; claim 8 discusses proportion of calories from yolk fat – distinct aspects of the same food."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":8,"rationale":"Both address whole‑egg composition but use different measures (fat percentages vs calorie source)."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":3,"toIndex":4,"rationale":"Claim 3 reports a correlation with reduced CAD risk; claim 4 reports no increase in heart‑disease risk – different outcome directions."},{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":4,"toIndex":9,"rationale":"Both state that moderate/one‑egg‑per‑day consumption does not raise heart‑disease/CVD risk; wording differs slightly in population description."},{"type":"equivalent","fromIndex":9,"toIndex":11,"rationale":"Both conclude that moderate daily egg consumption shows no increased cardiovascular disease risk."},{"type":"possibly-equivalent","fromIndex":4,"toIndex":11,"rationale":"Claim 4 limits to healthy people, while claim 11 is a general review; the core finding (no increased risk) is the same."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":6,"toIndex":1,"rationale":"Claim 6 reports eggs raise total and LDL cholesterol; claim 1 reports dietary cholesterol raises the total‑to‑HDL ratio – related but different cholesterol metrics."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":6,"toIndex":2,"rationale":"Claim 6 concerns cholesterol changes after eating eggs; claim 2 describes the cholesterol content of an egg."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":7,"toIndex":12,"rationale":"Claim 7 links egg consumption to higher all‑cause and CVD mortality; claim 12 links >1 egg/day to modest CVD risk increase in diabetics – different outcomes and subpopulations."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":12,"toIndex":13,"rationale":"Claim 12 finds increased CVD risk among type‑2 diabetics; claim 13 finds no association in the general population."},{"type":"contradicts","fromIndex":3,"toIndex":13,"rationale":"Claim 3 says higher egg intake reduces CAD risk, whereas claim 13 reports no significant association with CVD risk; both refer to similar populations, so they cannot both be true."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":14,"toIndex":6,"rationale":"Claim 14 examines risk change when replacing an egg with whole grains; claim 6 measures cholesterol changes from egg consumption – distinct interventions and outcomes."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":15,"toIndex":16,"rationale":"Both discuss limitations of observational evidence, but claim 15 mentions residual confounding while claim 16 notes lack of causality; they address related but not identical issues."}]}
